input.user_input: Explain the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing and how it applies to commercial contracts.

actual:
The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is a fundamental principle in contract law which presumes that both parties to an agreement will act honestly, fairly, and in good faith toward one another. The core purpose of the covenant is to ensure that neither party does anything that would destroy or injure the right of the other party to receive the benefits (the "fruits") of the contract.

In the United States, this principle is recognized by the common law (as outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts) and is codified in the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), which governs the sale of goods and other commercial transactions. 

Here is how the implied covenant applies to commercial contracts:

### 1. Protecting the Benefit of the Bargain
The covenant prevents a party from engaging in conduct that, while not explicitly prohibited by the exact text of the contract, clearly undermines the spirit of the agreement. For example, if a commercial lease bases the rent on a percentage of the tenant's gross sales, the tenant cannot intentionally divert all its business to a neighboring storefront it also owns just to avoid paying rent. Even if the lease does not explicitly forbid opening a store next door, doing so to evade the rent obligation would likely breach the implied covenant.
